---
title: "NeuroCamp Practice"
subtitle: "Graded challenges: fill in the blanks, get instant feedback"
format: live-html
engine: knitr
toc: true
webr:
  packages:
    - dplyr
    - infer
live:
  show-hints: true
  show-solutions: true
---

{{< include ./_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}
{{< include ./_extensions/r-wasm/live/_gradethis.qmd >}}

::: callout-note
## How this page works
This page is **optional**: it's for fast finishers, after-session review, or a presenter's
preflight check. It is **not** part of the core 60 to 75 minute lesson, and no one needs to
finish all five.

Each challenge has **blanks** (`______`) to fill in. Run it, and you get **instant feedback**
on whether you got it right. Stuck? Use the **Hint** and **Solution** buttons. Everything
runs in your browser, nothing to install. (First run is slow while R loads.)

These are the harder "Going further" challenges from the lesson, now self-checking. Same
sheep stem-cell data: 18 sheep, stem-cell treatment (`esc`) vs control (`ctrl`), heart
pumping capacity `before` and `after`, with `change = after - before`.
:::

## Challenge 1 — Summarize each group

Get each group's **average** change and its **spread** (standard deviation). Fill in the
grouping column and the column you're averaging.

```{webr}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_summary
library(dplyr)
sheep <- read.csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jt14den/neurocamp-stats-viz/main/data/stem-cell.csv") |>
  mutate(change = after - before)
```

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_summary
sheep |>
  group_by(______) |>
  summarise(avg = mean(______), spread = sd(change))
```

::: {.hint exercise="ex_summary"}
The column that says which group a sheep is in is `trmt`. You want the average of `change`.
:::

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_summary
#| solution: true
sheep |>
  group_by(trmt) |>
  summarise(avg = mean(change), spread = sd(change))
```

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_summary
#| check: true
gradethis::grade_this_code()
```

## Challenge 2 — Did the groups start out the same?

A fair experiment needs the groups comparable *before* treatment. Run a t-test on the
**before** values across the two groups. Fill in the column being tested.

```{webr}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_baseline
library(dplyr)
sheep <- read.csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jt14den/neurocamp-stats-viz/main/data/stem-cell.csv") |>
  mutate(change = after - before)
```

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_baseline
t.test(______ ~ trmt, data = sheep)
```

::: {.hint exercise="ex_baseline"}
You're checking the starting values, so the column is `before`.
:::

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_baseline
#| solution: true
t.test(before ~ trmt, data = sheep)
```

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_baseline
#| check: true
gradethis::grade_this_code()
```

## Challenge 3 — Compute the p-value yourself

The shuffle gave us 1000 "luck" differences in `luck`, and our real difference is in
`real_diff`. The p-value is the fraction of luck differences at least as big as ours. Fill
in the column name.

```{webr}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_pvalue
library(dplyr)
library(infer)
sheep <- read.csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jt14den/neurocamp-stats-viz/main/data/stem-cell.csv") |>
  mutate(change = after - before)
real_diff <- sheep |> specify(change ~ trmt) |>
  calculate(stat = "diff in means", order = c("esc", "ctrl"))
luck <- sheep |> specify(change ~ trmt) |>
  hypothesize(null = "independence") |>
  generate(reps = 1000, type = "permute") |>
  calculate(stat = "diff in means", order = c("esc", "ctrl"))
```

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_pvalue
mean(luck$stat >= real_diff$______)
```

::: {.hint exercise="ex_pvalue"}
Both `luck` and `real_diff` store the number in a column called `stat`.
:::

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_pvalue
#| solution: true
mean(luck$stat >= real_diff$stat)
```

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_pvalue
#| check: true
gradethis::grade_this_code()
```

::: callout-warning
If this comes out as `0`, it does **not** mean the probability is zero. It means none of the
1000 shuffles beat the real difference, so the p-value is just **smaller than 1000 shuffles
can estimate**, roughly less than 1 in 1000. Report it as `< 0.001`, not `0`.
:::

## Challenge 4 — Does more data change the p-value? (a toy demo)

Pretend a larger independent study found the same pattern. We *fake* it by stacking the same
sheep 3 times into `big` (same averages, same spread, just copied rows). Run the t-test on
`big` and see what the p-value does. Fill in which data frame to use.

```{webr}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_n
library(dplyr)
sheep <- read.csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jt14den/neurocamp-stats-viz/main/data/stem-cell.csv") |>
  mutate(change = after - before)
big <- bind_rows(sheep, sheep, sheep)
```

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_n
t.test(change ~ trmt, data = ______)
```

::: {.hint exercise="ex_n"}
Use the tripled data frame, `big`. Compare its p-value to the original.
:::

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_n
#| solution: true
t.test(change ~ trmt, data = big)
```

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_n
#| check: true
gradethis::grade_this_code()
```

::: callout-warning
This is a **toy demonstration, not** a valid way to get more evidence. Copying the same rows
is not new data. More data only helps when it means more *independent* observations (more
actual sheep). The lesson: a small p-value can come from a big effect **or** just a big
sample, so always check the effect size too.
:::

## Challenge 5 — Be a skeptic: the median difference

The mean can be pulled around by one odd sheep, so a careful skeptic also looks at the
**median**. As a first step, calculate the observed *median* difference between the groups.
(The full robustness check shuffles the labels too, see the optional cell below.) Fill in the
statistic name.

```{webr}
#| setup: true
#| exercise: ex_median
library(dplyr)
library(infer)
sheep <- read.csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jt14den/neurocamp-stats-viz/main/data/stem-cell.csv") |>
  mutate(change = after - before)
```

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_median
sheep |>
  specify(change ~ trmt) |>
  calculate(stat = "______", order = c("esc", "ctrl"))
```

::: {.hint exercise="ex_median"}
It looks just like `"diff in means"`, but with `medians`.
:::

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_median
#| solution: true
sheep |>
  specify(change ~ trmt) |>
  calculate(stat = "diff in medians", order = c("esc", "ctrl"))
```

```{webr}
#| exercise: ex_median
#| check: true
gradethis::grade_this_code()
```

::: callout-note
## Optional: the full robustness check (not graded)
The exercise above computes the observed median difference. The complete check also shuffles
the labels 1000 times and counts how often chance produces a median gap this big. Just run
this one (a `0` here means "smaller than 1000 shuffles can measure," not zero):
:::

```{webr}
library(dplyr)
library(infer)
sheep <- read.csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jt14den/neurocamp-stats-viz/main/data/stem-cell.csv") |>
  mutate(change = after - before)

real_med <- sheep |> specify(change ~ trmt) |>
  calculate(stat = "diff in medians", order = c("esc", "ctrl"))
luck_med <- sheep |> specify(change ~ trmt) |>
  hypothesize(null = "independence") |>
  generate(reps = 1000, type = "permute") |>
  calculate(stat = "diff in medians", order = c("esc", "ctrl"))

mean(luck_med$stat >= real_med$stat)
```

---

*Companion to the [main lesson](index.html). Real data: a randomized stem-cell trial in
sheep (Menasché et al., via the openintro R package).*
